elements.sent-2411-007: On the other hand, for the infinte denomintor, we deduce an asymptotic of nonmodular infinite products and therefore prove Merca's conjecture when $ n $ greater than a constant.

elements.sent-2411-009: In 2012, Andrews and Merca investigated the truncated form of Euler's pentagonal number theorem and obtained the following result \cite[Lemma 1.2]{andrews2012truncated : \begin{equation}\label{trun-euler} \frac{ (-1)^{k-1} }{(q ; q)_{\infty}} \sum_{j=-k}^{k-1}(-1)^j q^{j(3 j+1) / 2}= (-1)^{k-1} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{q^{\binom{k}{2}+(k+1) n}}{(q ; q)_n}{n-1\brack k-1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{trun-euler} \frac{ (-1)^{k-1} }{(q ; q)_{\infty}} \sum_{j=-k}^{k-1}(-1)^j q^{j(3 j+1) / 2}= (-1)^{k-1} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{q^{\binom{k}{2}+(k+1) n}}{(q ; q)_n}{n-1\brack k-1}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-010: One can see the expansion of $ q^{n $ on the right hand side of \eqref{trun-euler is nonnegative, so that the following recurrence relation holds, \begin{equation}(-1)^{k-1} \sum_{j=0}^{k-1}(-1)^j \big(p(n-j(3 j+1) / 2)-p(n-j(3 j+5) / 2-1)\big)\geq 0.\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-053: Throughout this paper, we adopt standard notations and terminologies for $q$-series, see, for example, \cite{Basic .}
elements.sent-2411-055: The $q$-shifted factorial is denoted by \begin{equation}(a)_n=(a;q)_n= 1, \it if $n=0$, [2mm] (1-a)(1-aq)\cdots(1-aq^{n-1}), \it if $n\geq 1$. \end{equation} We also use the notation \begin{equation}(a)_\infty=(a;q)_\infty=\prod_{n=0}^\infty (1-aq^n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-056: There are more compact notations for the multiple $q$-shifted factorials \begin{equation}(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_m;q)_n =(a_1;q)_n(a_2;q)_n \cdots(a_m;q)_n, [8pt] (a_1,a_2,\dots,a_m;q)_{\infty} =(a_1;q)_{\infty}(a_2;q)_{\infty}\cdots (a_m;q)_{\infty}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-057: The well-known Jacobi triple product identity is given by \cite[II.28]{Basic \begin{equation}\label{JTPI} \sum_{k=-\infty}^\infty (-1)^k q^{k\choose 2} a^k =(a,q/a,q;q)_\infty.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-430: The key idea of this method is to write the function under consideration into an integral over a circle, then transforming the integral into something more tractable to derive the asymptotic formula for the coefficient of the function.
